content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

March 11, 2020

Charlotte Craven
Camarillo City Councilmember
2238 Via del Suelo
Camarillo, CA 93010

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-20-030

Dear Ms. Craven:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090.

Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice. Because your inquiry is general in nature and does not pertain to a specific governmental decision, we are treating it as one for informal assistance.²

QUESTION

May you take part in decisions involving the City of Camarillo's trolley, given that one of the trolley stops is located within 500 feet from rental properties you own?

CONCLUSION

In general, it is presumed that the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a governmental decisions involving property located less than 500 feet from your real property is material, thus prohibiting you from taking part in such decisions in the absence of clear and convincing evidence that the decision will not have any measurable impact on your real property. In addition, where a decision solely concerns repairs, replacement, or maintenance of certain city facilities, the financial

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

File No. I-20-030
Page No. 2

effect of the decision on your real property is not considered material and you would not be
prohibited from taking part in such decisions.

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

You are a member of the Camarillo City Council. The City of Camarillo ("City") operates a
free-to-ride trolley as part of the City's transit system, which also includes a fixed route bus and a
"dial-a-ride" fleet. One of the trolley's ten stops is within 500 feet of rental properties you own. The
trolley stop near your property is in a shopping center and there is a six-foot-high cinder block wall
between the residences on that street and the shopping center, such that a person has to walk almost
half a mile from your property to reach the trolley stop.

During a follow-up phone call, you clarified that you are not currently faced with a specific
governmental decision involving the City's trolley, but you are seeking advice as to whether recusal
is necessary with respect to every governmental decision involving the trolley, given the proximity
of the trolley stop to your real property.

ANALYSIS

Economic Interests

Section 87100 of the Act provides that "[n]o public official at any level of state or local
government shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to
influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial
interest." Section 87103 further specifies that "[a] public official has a financial interest in a
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the
official, a member of his or her immediate family," or on certain enumerated economic interests.

As pertinent to the facts provided, those economic interests include "[a]ny business entity in
which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth two thousand dollars ($2,000) or
more," "[a]ny real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth two
thousand dollars ($2,000) or more," and "[a]ny source of income . . . aggregating five hundred
dollars ($500) or more in value provided or promised to, [or] received by, the public official within
12 months prior to the time when the decision is made." (Section 87103(b).) Accordingly, you have
potentially disqualifying economic interests in your rental business as a business entity and source
of income, your tenants as sources of income, and the rental properties.3

Reasonable Foreseeability

From the facts provided, it does not appear that your economic interests will be explicitly
involved in governmental decisions pertaining to the City's trolley as the interest are not a named

3 We note that you also have an interest in your personal finances. However, under Regulation 18702.5(c), a
decision's financial effect on an official's interest in his or her personal finances is not considered separately if the
decision affects the official's business interest or real property interest. Accordingly, we do not analyze any effect on
your personal finances further.

File No. I-20-030
Page No. 3

party in, or the subject of, the proceedings and the decisions not affect property as specified in
Regulation 18702.2(a)(1)(6). (Regulation 18701(b)) provides the standard for determining whether a financial effect is reasonably foreseeable when an official's economic
interest is not explicitly involved in a decision. The regulation states, "[a] financial effect need not
be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable. In general, if the financial effect can be
recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably
foreseeable. If the financial result cannot be expected absent extraordinary circumstances not
subject to the public official's control, it is not reasonably foreseeable."

Source of Income

Regulation 18702.3 provides the standard for determining whether a reasonably foreseeable
financial effect on an official's source of income is material. Among the instances in which such an
effect will be considered material is where the decision may affect the individual's income,
investments, or other assets or liabilities (other than an interest in a business entity or real property)
by $1,000 or more. (Regulation 18702.3(a)(2)(A).) An effect on an individual who is a source of
income may also be material if the official knows or has reason to know that the individual has an
interest in a business entity or real property that may be affected by the decision.

Business Entities

Regulation 18702.1 provides, in relevant part, that the reasonably foreseeable financial
effect of a governmental decision on an official's financial interest in a business entity, involving a
business entity that is a source of income to the official, is material where the decision may result in
an increase or decrease of the entity's annual gross revenues, or the value of the entity's assets or
liabilities, in an amount equal to or greater than $1,000,000, or five percent of the entity's annual
gross revenues and at least $10,000. (Regulations 18702.1(a)(2)(A)-(B), 18702.3(a)(4).) The
reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a governmental decision on an official's business entity
interest is also material where the decision may cause the entity to incur, avoid, reduce, or eliminate
expenses equal to or greater than $250,000, or one percent of the entity's annual gross revenues and
at least $2,500. (Regulation 18702.1(a)(3)(B).)

Real Property

Regulation 18702.2 contains the standards for determining when a governmental decision's
financial effect on an official's real property is "material." With respect to governmental decisions
involving the trolley and trolley stop near your properties, in general, the most relevant provision
will be subdivision (a)(7). Regulation 18702.2(a)(7) provides that "[t]he reasonably foreseeable
financial effect of a governmental decision on a parcel of real property in which an official has a
financial interest, other than a leasehold interest, is material whenever the governmental decision . . .
[i]nvolves property located 500 feet or less from the parcel unless there is clear
and convincing evidence that the decision will not have any measurable impact on the official's
property."

Note, however, that Regulation 18702.2(d)(1) states that "[t]he financial effect of a
governmental decision on a parcel of real property in which an official has a financial interest is not

File No. I-20-030
Page No. 4

material if . . . [t]he decision solely concerns repairs, replacement or maintenance of existing streets,
water, sewer, storm drainage or similar facilities."

Analysis

Without a specific governmental decision at issue, we can only provide general information
regarding potentially relevant conflict of interest provisions under the Act. Because the question of
whether a conflict of interest exists is considered on a decision-by-decision basis, it is not feasible
to determine whether you may or may not take part in any and all decisions involving the City's
trolley.

The statutes and regulations noted above will likely be the most relevant provisions to
consider when determining whether you may take part in a governmental decision involving the
trolley or trolley stop located near your rental properties. With specific regard to the "500-foot
standard," we note that a decision involving the City's trolley does not inherently involve property
within 500 feet of your rental units, such that the standard may not be applicable to some decisions.
In instances where the governmental decision would have an effect on the trolley stop located near
your rental units, the 500-foot standard will be implicated and require clear and convincing
evidence to overcome the presumption of a material financial effect on your real property. Again,
however, we note that Regulation 18702.2(d)(1) establishes an exception to the general materiality
standards where the governmental decision solely concerns repairs, replacement, or maintenance of
certain city facilities, which may include the City's trolley stop.

As a final note, we emphasize that the discussion above has merely highlighted the
provisions that readily appear to be potentially relevant to decisions involving the trolley, based on
the limited information provided. When dealing with a specific governmental decision, additional
facts will determine whether those provisions (or others not discussed in this letter) are applicable.
Whether you will be disqualified from a governmental decision involving the City's trolley or the
trolley stop near your rental properties is a highly fact-specific inquiry. Thus, you should contact the
FPPC for additional advice if and when you are faced with a specific governmental decision
regarding the City's trolley that potentially involves a disqualifying conflict of interest.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By: Kevin Cornwall
Counsel, Legal Division

KMC:aja

effect of the decision on your real property is not considered material and you would not be

Regulation 18702.1 provides, in relevant part, that the reasonably foreseeable financial
